title: Social Proof: When Everyone Shares, Does That Make It True?
titleAr: الدليل الاجتماعي: عندما يشارك الجميع، هل هذا يعني أنه صحيح؟
learningObjective: After this exercise, the user will be able to identify social proof manipulation (Cialdini, 2001) in online content, explain why high share/like counts do not indicate accuracy, and resist the bandwagon effect when evaluating claims.
learningObjectiveAr: بعد هذا التمرين، سيكون المستخدم قادرًا على تحديد التلاعب بالدليل الاجتماعي وشرح لماذا لا تشير أعداد المشاركات والإعجابات العالية إلى الدقة.

content.scenario:
Cialdini (2001) identified 'social proof' as one of the 6 universal principles of influence: people assume that if many others are doing something, it must be correct. In the digital world, this manifests as equating popularity (likes, shares, views) with truth.

Vosoughi, Roy & Aral (2018) in Science demonstrated the devastating consequence: false news stories are 70% more likely to be retweeted than true stories. False news reaches 1,500 people 6x faster than true news. Popularity ≠ Accuracy.

Scenario: Four posts about the same health topic appear on your social media feed. Which factors should influence your credibility assessment?
content.scenarioAr:
حدد شيالديني (2001) 'الدليل الاجتماعي' كأحد المبادئ الستة العالمية للتأثير: يفترض الناس أنه إذا كان الكثيرون يفعلون شيئاً ما، فلا بد أنه صحيح.

أثبت فوسوغي وروي وأرال (2018) في Science أن الأخبار الكاذبة أكثر احتمالاً لإعادة التغريد بنسبة 70% من الأخبار الصحيحة.

task.instructions: Evaluate each post. Select the ones that use social proof manipulation — where popularity metrics are being used to substitute for evidence.
task.instructionsAr: قيّم كل منشور. اختر المنشورات التي تستخدم التلاعب بالدليل الاجتماعي.

items.text: Post with 50K shares: 'This natural remedy cured my diabetes in 3 days! 50,000 people have already shared this — they can't all be wrong!' ← Uses share count as proof of truth.
items.explanation: SOCIAL PROOF MANIPULATION: The logical fallacy 'argumentum ad populum' — 50,000 shares prove only that 50,000 people clicked share, NOT that the content is true. Vosoughi et al. (2018) showed false health claims spread faster because they trigger stronger emotions.

items.text: WHO official account, 200 shares: 'New clinical trial data shows [treatment] reduces symptoms by 23% (p<0.01). Full study: [link to Lancet]' ← Uses evidence, not popularity, as the basis for the claim.
items.explanation: NOT MANIPULATIVE: This post cites specific data (23%, p<0.01), links to a peer-reviewed source (Lancet), and comes from an authoritative account (WHO). The low share count does NOT reduce its credibility.

items.text: Influencer post, 100K likes: 'Everyone I know is using this supplement. 100K likes can't be wrong. Don't be the last one to try it!' ← Classic bandwagon + social proof + FOMO.
items.explanation: TRIPLE MANIPULATION: Combines social proof (100K likes), bandwagon effect (everyone is doing it), and FOMO (don't be the last). Zero evidence presented. Cialdini (2001) warns this combination is especially persuasive.

items.text: Research post, 15 shares: 'Meta-analysis of 12 RCTs (N=4,500) shows no significant benefit of [supplement] vs placebo. doi:10.xxxx' ← Low shares, high evidence quality.
items.explanation: NOT MANIPULATIVE: A meta-analysis of 12 randomized controlled trials is among the highest quality evidence possible. 15 shares reflects that scientific findings don't go viral, not that they're less true.

feedback.correct: You correctly identified that posts 1 and 3 use social proof manipulation while posts 2 and 4 rely on evidence. The critical lesson: popularity and truth are INVERSELY correlated for health misinformation (Vosoughi et al., 2018). The viral post with 50K shares is less credible than the meta-analysis with 15 shares.
feedback.correctAr: لقد حددت بشكل صحيح أن المنشورين 1 و 3 يستخدمان التلاعب بالدليل الاجتماعي. الدرس الحاسم: الشعبية والحقيقة مترابطتان عكسياً في المعلومات الصحية المضللة.
feedback.incorrect: Remember: share counts, likes, and views measure POPULARITY, not ACCURACY. A post with 100K likes and zero evidence is less credible than a post with 15 shares and a peer-reviewed citation.
feedback.incorrectAr: تذكر: أعداد المشاركات والإعجابات والمشاهدات تقيس الشعبية، وليس الدقة.

whatNotToDo: Don't use share counts or likes as evidence that something is true — Vosoughi et al. (2018, Science) showed false news spreads 6x faster than true news
Don't assume that low-engagement posts are less credible — scientific findings rarely go viral
evidence: Cialdini, R. B. (2001). Influence: Science and Practice (4th ed.). Allyn & Bacon.; Vosoughi, S., Roy, D., & Aral, S. (2018). The spread of true and false news online. Science, 359(6380), 1146-1151.
safetyNote: This exercise uses fictional social media posts for educational purposes. For health advice, consult a licensed healthcare professional.
com_b_mechanism: Social proof literacy builds reflective resistance to bandwagon heuristic
